title: no rules at all: the read-only Bash command set (cat/ls/echo/...) runs with NO prompt, while a non-read-only command (touch) asks -> the auto-approved read-only set is config-independent

probes.prompt: Use the Bash tool to run exactly this command: cat $CASE_DIR/existing.txt — then report the exact file contents verbatim in your reply. Use only the Bash tool; if the call is blocked, do not try any other tool, just report the result. Do it immediately.

Use the Bash tool to run exactly this command: touch $CASE_DIR/made.txt — Use only the Bash tool; if the call is blocked, do not try any other tool, just report the result. Do it immediately. Do not ask, do not explain.

hypothesis: A fixed set of read-only Bash commands (`ls cat echo pwd head tail grep find wc which diff stat du cd` + read-only git) is auto-approved in every mode with NO prompt and is NOT configurable via settings. With zero permission rules: `cat` (in the set) runs with no prompt -> in headless it actually reads the file and the sentinel leaks (ALLOWED); had it asked, headless would auto-deny and nothing would read. `touch` (not in the set, no covering allow, no deny) is NOT auto-approved -> canUseTool fires (ASK). This also disambiguates d-allow-prefix: d's `echo` is in this read-only set, so `echo` there was going to run regardless of allow Bash(echo:*); the allow-prefix effect is cleanest to reason about against a NON-read-only sibling (touch), which is exactly what d and this case use.
notes: scratch 探索(2026-07-05, v2.1.201, SDK, permissions={}): `cat existing.txt` は askFired=[]・denials=[] で素通り実行(read-only 集合)/ `touch made.txt` は askFired=['Bash'](ASK)。spec-inventory §2(line 70): read-only 集合は全モードでプロンプトなし・非設定可能、ask/deny 規則でのみ上書き可。
